content: I think they spoof being Airbnb related or vrbo, etc. They put up 25-30 stolen fake listings, and the calendar is only available for future dates. You reserve an Airbnb for 3 months from now, but you're paying the reservation in advance. You're not paying it to Airbnb, you're paying to the scammer. If he gets 20 people paying for a future reservation  of 1200+, you're making 20k. By the time you go to your reservation, they're long gone!

content: I wonder if it might perhaps just be simple credit fraud/money laundering. The scammer could potentially be using stolen cards to book stays under their own fake listings and cashing out as soon as the funds become available. Anyone else that happens to get caught up would just be collateral damage, so to  speak, and in for a frustrating but relatively minor inconvenience.

content: Ok, but that would require somehow "breaking into" a real account (one created by an actual property owner), as opposed to the low-effort act of creating listings out of thin air...

content:
Yes, on AirBnB I much prefer to book properties that have had at least some reviews (and, ideally, recent reviews); this is a good way to avoid listings that are "not real". (Not necessarily intentional fakes, but more of a kind, "Oh... yeah, I played around with the airbnb web site a while back... never realized I am actually set up to accept reservations!" I've seen that both in the USA and in the Philippines a few times). 

That being said, for every listing someone has to be the first guest :-)   Sometimes, the only suitable listing at your desired location is a new one. And sometimes a new listing turns out to be a great one.

The other platform, United Hotels, I have not used before, and probably would not have used now either, if not for some promotion from my credit card. Being unfamiliar with that site, I guess I just concentrated on the description and location, and ignored the absence of rating and reviews.

content: There was hotel we used to visit as a kid and for nostalgia’s sake I wanted to book the same a few years ago. Went to check Tr#padvisot for reviews and found quite a few good ones. The website also took me to another booking platform and the rates were pretty good. The hotel was part of a global chain and I thought why not check if they have better prices. Turns out that the  hotel had been shut down over a decade ago and I verified the same using Google.

content: It's not that difficult. Con artists routinely use social engineering tactics to trick owners of existing accounts into handing over access to those accounts. See for example the !discord scam, or they might just call and claim to be "tech support" or something. There are all sorts of different ways they might fool account owners into giving away their accounts, most of which wouldn't involve any kind of "hacking" as you might think of it.

content: If you are the first guest, converse with the host. Fraudsters generally don't talk. Ask about things like check in process and nearby restaurants. Real hosts will say something human-like.

content:
Hi /u/cstaub67, AutoModerator has been summoned to explain the Steam or Discord fake report scam.

This is a very common scam that happens either on Steam or Discord, or both.

You receive a message from someone claiming that they accidentally reported you, and they will direct you to the profile of a scammer that impersonates a Steam or Discord employee or admin. The other account will then either try to steal your Steam or Discord account. If you encounter this scam, ignore the scammers and report their accounts, including the one that says they accidentally reported you.

Remember: [the only way to contact Steam support is clicking here](https://help.steampowered.com/en/), and [the only way to contact Discord support is clicking here](https://support.discord.com/hc/en-us/requests/new).

[Here](https://i.imgur.com/DIgBZIN.png) is an example of the scam, [here](https://blog.malwarebytes.com/scams/2021/03/steam-users-dont-fall-for-the-i-accidentally-reported-you-scam/) is an article about the scam, and [here](https://help.steampowered.com/en/faqs/view/3195-9FFB-BA06-F25B) is Steam's scam page.


*I am a bot, and this action was performed automatically. Please [contact the moderators of this subreddit](/message/compose/?to=/r/Scams) if you have any questions or concerns.*

content:
The company is real, but you're not talking to anyone from there. They are impersonators trying to scam people. Everyone in the group chat is also in on it. (they will show fake payment screenshots and praise them for "hiring" them)

These task scams take you on a different site and use crypto. It's not a "work site". It's a fake site made by these scammers, which is why it's not the actual domain. Your "mentor" will be instructing you along the way to show fake profits.

Often, yes, they will pay out a little bit to gain your trust for the sunk cost fallacy. They want you to put more money towards it for higher rewards, only eventually your account will go negative. To get back up? PAY them of course!   Read up on "task scams" with the automod text bot and these links:

Official government warnings and info:

➜ [ic3.gov/PSA/2024/PSA240604](http://ic3.gov/PSA/2024/PSA240604)

➜ [ftc.gov/consumer-alerts/2024/11/task-scams-create-illusion-making-money](http://ftc.gov/consumer-alerts/2024/11/task-scams-create-illusion-making-money)

➜ [fbi.gov/how-we-can-help-you/victim-services/national-crimes-and-victim-resources/cryptocurrency-job-scams](http://fbi.gov/how-we-can-help-you/victim-services/national-crimes-and-victim-resources/cryptocurrency-job-scams)

Get out now and block EVERYONE.
